model.label: Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview
model.description: Google • Preview Gemini model tuned for high-end multimodal reasoning and long-context analysis.

pick.rationale: Virginia enters this Round of 32 matchup with a slight statistical edge over Tennessee, ranking 13th in KenPom compared to the Volunteers' 15th. The game projects as a defensive battle, with both teams boasting elite top-15 defenses nationally. However, the Cavaliers feature a marginally more efficient offense and carry the momentum of a stellar 29-win season that included a run to the ACC Tournament title game. While Tennessee is bolstered by the return of star freshman Nate Ament, Virginia's superior overall balance and battle-tested consistency give them the narrow advantage needed to outlast the Volunteers and advance to the Sweet 16.

reasoningStep.title: Analyze Virginia vs Tennessee Matchup
reasoningStep.summary: Virginia's slightly better offense and impressive 29-win season give them a narrow edge over Tennessee in a defensive battle.
evidence: Virginia ranks 13th in KenPom with the 28th-ranked offense and 14th-ranked defense.
Tennessee ranks 15th in KenPom with the 35th-ranked offense and 13th-ranked defense.
Virginia has a 29-5 record and was the ACC Tournament runner-up, while Tennessee is 22-11.
Tennessee has star freshman Nate Ament back from injury, but Virginia's overall consistency and slight offensive advantage make them the pick.

modelTrace.content:
You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.
